# Native App Automation Testing Software: Best Tools & How to Choose the Right One

[![Crissy Joshua](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*HeBMI9SfPtqLdBbPkuY27A.png)](https://medium.com/@crissyjoshua?source=post_page---byline--091356ce2e6b---------------------------------------)

[Crissy Joshua](https://medium.com/@crissyjoshua?source=post_page---byline--091356ce2e6b---------------------------------------)

Follow

Share

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*XG7SCkm8q0HxA6GJ)

Photo by [Joan Gamell](https://unsplash.com/@gamell?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

Testing native mobile apps manually is a very time taking process and QA teams face a lot of issues. I have been in situations where a simple app update had to be checked across multiple Android and iOS devices, different OS versions, and edge cases like permissions, network drops, etc. This increases the risk of missing important bugs.

Testing the same app on multiple Android and iOS devices, with different screen sizes and OS versions, simply doesn’t work well manually. Industry reports show that around 50–60% of mobile defects are linked to device or OS issues. So, here comes the usage of native app automation testing software. It helps teams run the same tests perfectly across devices, catch issues early, and reduce dependency on frequent manual checks. Read this blog to learn more about it and some of the best native app automation testing tools in the industry.

## What is Native App Automation Testing?

Native app automation testing is the process of validating Android and iOS apps using automated codes that directly checks with native UI elements and device features. These tests run on real devices or emulators to check real user actions.

It works entirely different than mobile web testing, native automation works closely with platform based components such as OS APIs, permissions, notifications, and hardware. This makes it important for catching issues that only appear in real app usage, not in a browser based environment.

## Why Automate Testing for Native Apps?

Here are some of the reasons from my end for doing automated testing for native apps:

- In one of my past projects, regression testing that took almost two days manually was reduced to under an hour after automating core flows. In most teams, automation cuts regression time by 60% or more.
- Most of the mobile bugs are device or OS specific. Automation makes it possible to test the same flows across multiple Android and iOS versions.
- CI automation generally catches crashes and broken flows before QA starts testing. Teams detect 30 to 40% more defects this way.
- Stable automation reduces release risk. I have seen teams release faster once smoke and regression tests were automated.

## Top Native App Automation Testing Tools

Let’s check out some of the best native app automation testing tool along with their key features:

### 1\. BrowserStack App Automate

BrowserStack App Automate is a cloud based platform for running native and hybrid app automation tests on real Android and iOS devices without any lab maintenance. It gives instant access to thousands of real devices, supports major test frameworks like Appium, Espresso, and XCUITest, and integrates easily with existing CI/CD pipelines.

**Best Features:**

- You can easily test on 30,000+ real Android and iOS devices and OS versions to catch issues.
- You can run tests in parallel across devices to reduce overall test time.
- There are built-in AI agents like self healing, test selection, and failure analysis to speed up debugging.
- Test advanced real device features such as biometric authentication, network conditions, and payment flows on real devices.

**My Thoughts:**

BrowserStack App Automate works best when device coverage and execution speed become real challenges. I like that it removes the effort of managing devices while still keeping tests close to real world conditions. Features like AI agents lowers a lot of testing issues, mainly in large test cases.

### 2\. Appium

Appium is an open source automation software for testing native, hybrid, and mobile web applications across Android and iOS using the WebDriver protocol. It allows you to write tests in your preferred language without modifying the app itself, making it flexible and powerful for mobile automation.

**Best Features:**

- You can write one test suite and run it on both Android and iOS devices.
- Easily use Java, Python, JavaScript, Ruby, C#, and more based on your team’s skills.
- Comes free with a large community contributing drivers, plugins, and updates.
- It works perfectly with your app’s existing binaries without extra modification.

**My Thoughts:**

Appium is a good choice when you want full control and cross platform automation without paying for a tool. It works well if the team invests time in building a stable framework. Without following this, tests can become harder to maintain.

### 3\. XCUITest

XCUITest is Apple’s native UI automation software built into XCTest and Xcode for testing iOS applications. It allows you to write UI automation tests in Swift or Objective‑C and interact with app screens like a real user through tapping, swiping, and typing. Because it’s part of Apple’s ecosystem, setup is simple and perfectly integrated with development workflows.

**Best Features:**

- It provides direct access to iOS APIs and accessibility identifiers that ensures tests are correct.
- It offers interaction with elements, gestures, and alerts exactly like a user would.
- It works smoothly with Xcode Server, Jenkins, and other CI tools for automated pipelines.
- This tool provides detailed logs, screenshots, and error messages to quickly identify test failures.

**My Thoughts:**

XCUITest is ideal for iOS teams needing stable, reliable automation within Apple’s ecosystem. It is easy to set up and integrates well with CI/CD, but it’s limited to iOS and need macOS/Xcode.

### 4\. Espresso

Espresso is Google’s native UI automation software for Android, designed to write fast and reliable UI tests directly within Android Studio. It checks real user actions like clicks, text input, and gestures while automatically synchronizing with the app’s UI interface. For teams focused on Android only automation, it’s one of the most efficient and developer friendly choices available.

**Best Features:**

- Espresso waits for the UI to be idle before performing actions to reduce incorrect tests.
- Runs tests in the same process as the app, giving quicker feedback than client-server frameworks.
- Simple core API with matchers, actions, and assertions makes tests readable and maintainable.
- Works perfectly with Android Studio, Gradle, and CI/CD pipelines for smooth automation workflows.

**My Thoughts:**

I find Espresso excellent for Android only teams, especially when fast, reliable UI feedback is important. It is perfectly integrated and feels natural when working inside Android Studio, though automation skills must align with Android development.

### 5\. Calabash

Calabash is an open source mobile automation software that supports behaviour driven development, which allows you to write tests in simple language (Gherkin). It works with both Android and iOS apps by interacting through predefined steps and can be useful when collaboration between teams is important. While Calabash was popular in earlier years, now it is not used very widely.

**Best Features:**

- It uses Gherkin syntax, which makes tests readable for technical and non-technical team members.
- Can automate both Android and iOS tests using the same test definitions.
- It is completely free to use with a strong community support.
- Test cases written in natural language help to collaborate easily with QA, developers, and product owners.

**My Thoughts:**

It is a good tool for teams that value shared understanding between QA and business, but its declining updates make it less practical for long term automation stacks. In modern projects, tools like BrowserStack Automate and Appium provide better performance and ecosystem support.

### 6\. TestComplete

TestComplete is a paid automated testing platform from SmartBear that supports functional UI testing across desktop, web, and mobile applications, including Android and iOS. It allows you to create automated mobile tests using both code based and codeless method, with built-in support for real devices or cloud device farms like BitBar. Its flexibility make it a full stack option for teams that want one tool for multiple application types.

**Best Features:**

- Record and playback or write tests in languages like JavaScript, Python, and VBScript.
- Automate Android and iOS apps with desktop and web tests from a single suite.
- You can run tests in parallel on multiple devices through cloud integrations to speed up cycles.
- Advanced UI element detection helps maintain stability even as app UI changes.

**My Thoughts:**

TestComplete works perfectly as an all in one tool for mobile, web, and desktop automation without building multiple frameworks. It’s good for testers with mixed skill levels, but its licensing cost and occasional maintenance suits large teams more accurately.

## How to Set Up Native App Automation Testing?

**Step 1:** Select an automation tool like BrowserStack Automate or Espresso based on your app platform and team expertise.

**Step 2:** Prepare your local environment by installing the software, required SDKs, and language setups so that tests can run smoothly.

**Step 3:** Upload your .apk or .ipa file to BrowserStack App Automate and note the generated app URL.

**Step 4:** Define device, OS version, automation framework, and app URL in your test configuration.

**Step 5:** Connect to BrowserStack App Automate using credentials and start tests on real devices. Then, automate key user flows and run tests on real Android and iOS devices in the cloud.

**Step 6:** Connect BrowserStack with your CI/CD tools to run native app tests automatically on every build.

## How to Choose the Right Native App Automation Testing Software?

Some of the pointers which I would prefer to consider while selecting a native app automation testing software:

- I usually check how easy the tool is to set up and use, especially for teams new to automation.
- According to my experience, always select a platform that does real device testing on both Android and iOS.
- I look for smooth support for Appium, XCUITest, or Espresso so the team can reuse existing skills.
- Simple and accurate logs, screenshots, and videos make it much easier to understand failures. Advanced features like AI support, large coverage, etc. can be considered on priority too.
- I always make sure that the tool supports parallel runs and integrates well with CI/CD pipelines.

The creator's accounts are [`https://x.com/_takahirom_`](https://x.com/_takahirom_) and [`https://x.com/new_runnable`](https://x.com/new_runnable) . ## Screenshot [Permalink: Screenshot](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#screenshot) ![arbigent-screenshot](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1386930/404649541-77ebfcb1-3a44-4eaf-9775-3dff2597f9d1.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..J_ByROPtm4JOKOQJZh6_iDgFKpPPmGdQD72x2UqDUv8) ## Demo movie [Permalink: Demo movie](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#demo-movie) arbigent-demo.mp4 ## Motivation [Permalink: Motivation](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#motivation) ### Make AI Agent Testing Practical for Modern Applications [Permalink: Make AI Agent Testing Practical for Modern Applications](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#make-ai-agent-testing-practical-for-modern-applications) Traditional UI testing often relies on brittle methods that are easily disrupted by even minor UI changes. A/B tests, updated tutorials, unexpected dialogs, dynamic advertising, or ever-changing user-generated content can cause tests to fail. AI agents emerged as a solution, but testing with AI agents also presents challenges. AI agents often don't work as intended; for example, the agents might open other apps or click on the wrong button due to the complexity of the task. To address these challenges, I created Arbigent, an AI agent testing framework that can break down complex tasks into smaller, dependent scenarios. By decomposing tasks, Arbigent enables more predictable and scalable testing of AI agents in modern applications. ### Customizable for Various AI Providers, OSes, Form Factors, etc. [Permalink: Customizable for Various AI Providers, OSes, Form Factors, etc.](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#customizable-for-various-ai-providers-oses-form-factors-etc) I believe many AI Agent testing frameworks will emerge in the future. However, widespread adoption might be delayed due to limitations in customization. For instance: - **Limited AI Provider Support:** Frameworks might be locked to specific AI providers, excluding those used internally by companies. - **Slow OS Adoption:** Support for different operating systems (like iOS and Android) could lag. - **Delayed Form Factor Support:** Expanding to form factors beyond phones, such as Android TV, might take considerable time. To address these issues, I aimed to create a framework that empowers users with extensive customization capabilities. Inspired by [OkHttp's interceptor](https://square.github.io/okhttp/features/interceptors/) pattern, Arbigent provides interfaces for flexible customization, allowing users to adapt the framework to their specific needs, such as those listed above. ### Easy Integration into Development Workflows [Permalink: Easy Integration into Development Workflows](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#easy-integration-into-development-workflows) Furthermore, I wanted to make Arbigent accessible to QA engineers by offering a user-friendly UI. This allows for scenario creation within the UI and seamless test execution via the code interface. ## Key Feature Breakdown [Permalink: Key Feature Breakdown](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#key-feature-breakdown) **I. Core Functionality & Design** - **Complex Task Management:** - **Scenario Dependencies:** Breaks down complex goals into smaller, manageable scenarios that depend on each other (e.g., login -> search). - **Orchestration:** Acts as a mediator, managing the execution flow of AI agents across multiple, interconnected scenarios. - **Hybrid Development Workflow:** - **UI-Driven Scenario Creation:** Allows non-programmers (e.g., QA engineers) to visually design test scenarios through a user-friendly interface. - **Code-Based Execution:** Enables software engineers to execute the saved scenarios programmatically (YAML files), allowing for integration with existing testing infrastructure. **II. Cross-Platform & Device Support** - **Multi-Platform Compatibility:** - **Mobile & TV:** Supports testing on iOS, Android, Web, and TV interfaces. - **D-Pad Navigation:** Handles TV interfaces that rely on D-pad navigation. **III. AI Optimization & Efficiency** - **Enhanced AI Understanding:** - **UI Tree Optimization:** Simplifies and filters the UI tree to improve AI comprehension and performance. - **Accessibility-Independent:** Provides annotated screenshots to assist AI in understanding UIs that lack accessibility information. - **Cost Savings:** - **Open Source:** Free to use, modify, and distribute, eliminating licensing costs. - **Efficient Model Usage:** Defaults to `gpt-4.1` for improved capabilities; also supports cost-effective models like `gpt-4o-mini`. **IV. Robustness & Reliability** - **Double Check with AI-Powered Image Assertion:** Integrates [Roborazzi's feature](https://takahirom.github.io/roborazzi/ai-powered-image-assertion.html#behavior-of-ai-powered-image-assertion) to verify AI decisions using image-based prompts and allows the AI to re-evaluate if needed. - **Stuck Screen Detection:** Identifies and recovers from situations where the AI agent gets stuck on the same screen, prompting it to reconsider its actions. **V. Advanced Features & Customization** - **Flexible Code Interface:** - **Custom Hooks:** Offers a code interface for adding custom initialization and cleanup methods, providing greater control over scenario execution. - **Maestro YAML Integration:** - **Pre-defined Test Flows:** Execute existing Maestro YAML test flows as initialization methods within Arbigent scenarios. - **Reusable Test Components:** Leverage your existing Maestro test automation scripts to set up complex application states before AI agent testing. - **Example Use Cases:** - Run login flows using Maestro YAML before AI agent testing - Set up specific application states (e.g., user onboarding completion) - Execute complex setup sequences that require precise timing - Integrate existing Maestro test assets into AI agent testing workflows - **Model Context Protocol (MCP) Support:** - Introduced initial support for MCP, enabling Arbigent to leverage external tools and services defined via MCP servers. This significantly extends testing capabilities beyond direct UI interaction. - You can configure MCP servers using a JSON string in the Project Settings. - **Example MCP Use Cases:** - Install and launch applications - Check server logs (e.g., user behavior) using external tools - Retrieve debug logs - Interact with various other custom tools and services - **MCP Server Control:** - **Project-level defaults:** Use the `enabled` field in MCP JSON to disable servers by default: ``` { "mcpServers": { "filesystem": { "command": "npx", "args": ["..."] }, "github": { "command": "npx", "args": ["..."], "enabled": false } } } ``` - **Scenario-level overrides:** Override project defaults per scenario using `mcpOptions`: ``` scenarios: - id: "my-scenario" goal: "Test with specific MCP servers" mcpOptions: mcpServerOptions: - name: "github" enabled: true # Override: enable for this scenario - name: "filesystem" enabled: false # Override: disable for this scenario ``` - Servers without overrides use project defaults. If no `enabled` field exists, the server is enabled by default. - **AI Hints - App-Provided Context:** - Apps can provide domain-specific hints to help the AI understand the current screen better. - Embed hints in the accessibility label using the `[[aihint:...]]` format: ``` // Android (contentDescription) view.contentDescription = "Play button [[aihint:Video player, buffering]]" ``` ``` // iOS (accessibilityLabel) button.accessibilityLabel = "Play button [[aihint:Video player, buffering]]" ``` ``` ``` - The `[[...]]` format allows hints to coexist with accessibility labels and supports structured data like JSON. - **Note:** Since this uses accessibility labels, it affects screen readers (TalkBack, VoiceOver, etc.). Enable hints only during testing to avoid accessibility issues in production. **VI. Community & Open Source** - **Open Source Nature:** - **Free & Open:** Freely available for use, modification, and distribution. - **Community Driven:** Welcomes contributions from the community to enhance and expand the framework. ## Arbigent's Strengths and Weaknesses Based on [SMURF](https://testing.googleblog.com/2024/10/smurf-beyond-test-pyramid.html) [Permalink: Arbigent's Strengths and Weaknesses Based on SMURF](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#arbigents-strengths-and-weaknesses-based-on-smurf) I categorized automated testing frameworks into five levels using the [SMURF](https://testing.googleblog.com/2024/10/smurf-beyond-test-pyramid.html) framework. Here's how Arbigent stacks up: - **Speed (1/5):**Arbigent's speed is currently limited by the underlying AI technology and the need to interact with the application's UI in real-time. This makes it slower than traditional unit or integration tests. - We have introduced some mechanisms to address this: - Tests can be parallelized using the **`--shard` option** to speed up execution. - **AI result caching** can be utilized when the UI tree and goal are identical, which is configurable in the project settings. - **Maintainability (4/5):** Arbigent excels in maintainability. The underlying AI model can adapt to minor UI changes, minimizing the need to rewrite tests for every small update, thus reducing maintenance effort. You can write tests in natural language (e.g., "Complete the tutorial"), making them resilient to UI changes. The task decomposition feature also reduces duplication, further enhancing maintainability. Maintenance can be done by non-engineers, thanks to the natural language interface. - **Utilization (1/5):** Arbigent requires both device resources (emulators or physical devices) and AI resources, which can be costly. (AI cost can be around $0.005 per step and $0.02 per task when using GPT-4o.) - **Reliability (3/5):**Arbigent has several features to improve reliability. It automatically waits during loading screens, handles unexpected dialogs, and even attempts self-correction. However, external factors like emulator flakiness can still impact reliability. - Recently I found Arbigent has retry feature and can execute the scenario from the beginning. But, **even without retry, Arbigent works fine without failures thanks to the flexibility of AI.** - **Fidelity (5/5):** Arbigent provides high fidelity by testing on real or emulated devices with the actual application. It can even assess aspects that were previously difficult to test, such as verifying video playback by checking for visual changes on the screen. I believe that many of its current limitations, such as speed, maintainability, utilization, and reliability, will be addressed as AI technology continues to evolve. The need for extensive prompt engineering will likely diminish as AI models become more capable. ## How to Use [Permalink: How to Use](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#how-to-use) ### Installation [Permalink: Installation](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#installation) Install the Arbigent UI binary from the [Release page](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent/releases). ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1386930/401803100-499da604-4a43-4eb8-b27f-325a83caa013.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..kmZ85nXeYfb4fM0-03FBsOup06oEX5DdIANasgdh-r8) ### Installation for macOS Users [Permalink: Installation for macOS Users](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#installation-for-macos-users) If you encounter security warnings when opening the app: Refer to [Apple's guide on opening apps from unidentified developers](https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/open-a-mac-app-from-an-unknown-developer-mh40616/mac). ![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1386930/406661402-44372fa0-1f58-4eea-9fff-a7cc672a0f86.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..W0Y-RtwbTgO3WRxJHsVX0W5Ut7KS3tZLDR6YKRZueTc) This `Open Anyway` button is available for about an hour after you try to open the app. ![](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1386930/406660841-b8249cd6-3a40-4863-827c-78e63fdbfd87.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..jyNudD9b00nbEkQ39QMQ8LMB5d8pBm7ddR6Ot0FDZ9c) ### Device Connection and AI API Key Entry [Permalink: Device Connection and AI API Key Entry](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#device-connection-and-ai-api-key-entry) 1. Set up complex application states using deterministic Maestro flows - Reuse existing Maestro test automation assets - Combine precise setup sequences with AI-driven testing ### Test Execution [Permalink: Test Execution](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#test-execution) Run tests either directly through the UI or programmatically via the code interface or CLI. ### CLI [Permalink: CLI](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#cli) You can install the CLI via Homebrew and run a saved YAML file. ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1386930/400628431-f20fdac8-ae46-4dc7-aa2b-6fc0b5bd4a9d.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..9uUT5hjFfcruJh0MZ5Dk3CK1gtFhmf2_pf1anup4teg) ``` brew tap takahirom/homebrew-repo brew install takahirom/repo/arbigent ``` ``` Usage: arbigent [] []... Options: -h, --help Show this message and exit Commands: run Execute test scenarios scenarios List available scenarios tags Manage scenario tags ``` #### Configuration with Settings Files [Permalink: Configuration with Settings Files](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#configuration-with-settings-files) Arbigent supports configuration via settings files in the `.arbigent` directory, which simplifies CLI usage by eliminating the need to specify common parameters repeatedly. Create a `.arbigent` directory in your project root and place your configuration files inside. Arbigent supports multiple configuration files with automatic priority ordering - local configuration files have higher priority than global ones, allowing you to maintain separate settings for different environments while providing appropriate fallbacks. **Prioritized Configuration Support:** Arbigent supports both global and command-specific configurations with prioritization: 1. **High Priority**: Command-specific settings (e.g., `run.ai-type`) 2. **Low Priority**: Global settings (e.g., `ai-type`) \- used as fallback **Example hybrid configuration:** ``` # Global settings (shared across all commands) project-file: tests/arbigent-project.yml working-directory: /path/to/your/project log-level: info # Default AI configuration (fallback) ai-type: openai openai-api-key: sk-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx openai-model-name: gpt-4.1 # Run command specific settings (overrides global settings) run: ai-type: azureopenai # Overrides global ai-type azure-openai-endpoint: https://xxxxxxxxx.openai.azure.com/openai/deployments/xxxxx/ azure-openai-api-version: 2025-xx-xx azure-openai-model-name: gpt-4o azure-openai-api-key: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx os: android ``` **Simple flat configuration (still supported):** ``` ai-type: azureopenai azure-openai-endpoint: https://xxxxxxxxx.openai.azure.com/openai/deployments/xxxxx/ azure-openai-api-version: 2025-xx-xx azure-openai-model-name: gpt-4o azure-openai-api-key: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx project-file: tests/arbigent-project.yml os: android log-level: info working-directory: /path/to/your/project ``` When using a settings file, you can run tests with the simplified command: ``` arbigent run ``` All parameters configured in the settings files will be shown as `(source: already provided by property file)` in the help output, indicating which settings are already configured. #### arbigent run command [Permalink: arbigent run command](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#arbigent-run-command) ``` Usage: arbigent run [] Options for OpenAI API AI: --openai-endpoint= Endpoint URL (default: https://api.openai.com/v1/) --openai-model-name= Model name (default: gpt-4.1) --openai-api-key, --openai-key= API key Options for Gemini API AI: --gemini-endpoint= Endpoint URL (default: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/) --gemini-model-name= Model name (default: gemini-1.5-flash) ``` arbigent tags ``` ### Shard Option to Enable Parallel Tests [Permalink: Shard Option to Enable Parallel Tests](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#shard-option-to-enable-parallel-tests) You can run tests separately with the `--shard` option. This allows you to split your test suite and run tests in parallel, reducing overall test execution time. **Example:** ``` arbigent run --shard=1/4 ``` This command will run the first quarter of your test suite. #### Running Specific Scenarios [Permalink: Running Specific Scenarios](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#running-specific-scenarios) You can run specific scenarios by their IDs: ``` arbigent run --scenario-ids="scenario-1,scenario-2" ``` Or filter scenarios by tags: ``` arbigent run --tags="smoke,regression" ``` **Integrating with GitHub Actions:** Here's an example of how to integrate the `--shard` option with GitHub Actions to run parallel tests on multiple Android emulators: ``` cli-e2e-android: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: fail-fast: false matrix: shardIndex: [ 1, 2, 3, 4 ] shardTotal: [ 4 ] steps: ... - name: CLI E2E test uses: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2 ... script: | arbigent run --shard=${{ matrix.shardIndex }}/${{ matrix.shardTotal }} --os=android --project-file=sample-test/src/main/resources/projects/e2e-test-android.yaml --ai-type=gemini --gemini-model-name=gemini-2.0-flash-exp ... - uses: actions/upload-artifact@b4b15b8c7c6ac21ea08fcf65892d2ee8f75cf882 # v4 if: ${{ always() }} with: name: cli-report-android-${{ matrix.shardIndex }}-${{ matrix.shardTotal }} path: | arbigent-result/* retention-days: 90 ``` ### Minimal GitHub Actions CI sample [Permalink: Minimal GitHub Actions CI sample](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#minimal-github-actions-ci-sample) You can use the CLI in GitHub Actions like in this sample. There are only two files: `.github/workflows/arbigent-test.yaml` and `arbigent-project.yaml`. This example demonstrates GitHub Actions and an `arbigent-project.yaml` file created by the Arbigent UI. [https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent-sample](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent-sample) ## Supported AI Providers [Permalink: Supported AI Providers](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#supported-ai-providers) | AI Provider | Supported | | --- | --- | | OpenAI | Yes | | Gemini | Yes | | OpenAI based APIs like Ollama | Yes | You can add AI providers by implementing the `ArbigentAi` interface. ## Supported OSes / Form Factors [Permalink: Supported OSes / Form Factors](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#supported-oses--form-factors) | OS | Supported | Test Status in the Arbigent repository | | --- | --- | --- | | Android | Yes | End-to-End including Android emulator and real AI | | iOS | Yes | End-to-End including iOS simulator and real AI | | Web(Chrome) | Yes | Currently, Testing not yet conducted | You can add OSes by implementing the `ArbigentDevice` interface. Thanks to the excellent [Maestro](https://github.com/mobile-dev-inc/maestro) library, we are able to support multiple OSes. | Form Factor | Supported | | --- | --- | | Phone / Tablet | Yes | | TV(D-Pad) | Yes | # Learn More [Permalink: Learn More](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#learn-more) ## Basic Structure [Permalink: Basic Structure](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#basic-structure) ### Execution Flow [Permalink: Execution Flow](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#execution-flow) The execution flow involves the UI, Arbigent, ArbigentDevice, and ArbigentAi. The UI sends a project creation request to Arbigent, which fetches the UI tree from ArbigentDevice. ArbigentAi then decides on an action based on the goal and UI tree. The action is performed by ArbigentDevice, and the results are returned to the UI for display. Render ArbigentAiArbigentDeviceArbigentAgentUI(or Tests)ArbigentAiArbigentDeviceArbigentAgentUI(or Tests)loop​ExecuteFetch UI treeReturn UI treeDecide Action by goal and UI tree and historiesReturn ActionPerform actionsReturn resultsDisplay results Loading ``` sequenceDiagram participant UI(or Tests) participant ArbigentAgent participant ArbigentDevice participant ArbigentAi UI(or Tests)->>ArbigentAgent: Execute loop ArbigentAgent->>ArbigentDevice: Fetch UI tree ArbigentDevice->>ArbigentAgent: Return UI tree ArbigentAgent->>ArbigentAi: Decide Action by goal and UI tree and histories ArbigentAi->>ArbigentAgent: Return Action ArbigentAgent->>ArbigentDevice: Perform actions ArbigentDevice->>ArbigentAgent: Return results end ArbigentAgent->>UI(or Tests): Display results ``` ### Class Diagram [Permalink: Class Diagram](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#class-diagram) The class diagram illustrates the relationships between ArbigentProject, ArbigentScenario, ArbigentTask, ArbigentAgent, ArbigentScenarioExecutor, ArbigentAi, ArbigentDevice, and ArbigentInterceptor. Render \* \* \* \* ArbigentProject +List scenarios +execute() ArbigentAgentTask +String goal ArbigentAgent +ArbigentAi ai +ArbigentDevice device +List interceptors +execute(arbigentAgentTask) ArbigentScenarioExecutor +execute(arbigentScenario) ArbigentScenario +List agentTasks Loading ``` classDiagram direction TB class ArbigentProject { +List~ArbigentScenario~ scenarios +execute() } class ArbigentAgentTask { +String goal } class ArbigentAgent { +ArbigentAi ai +ArbigentDevice device +List~ArbigentInterceptor~ interceptors +execute(arbigentAgentTask) } class ArbigentScenarioExecutor { +execute(arbigentScenario) } class ArbigentScenario { +List~ArbigentAgentTask~ agentTasks } ArbigentProject o--"*" ArbigentScenarioExecutor ArbigentScenarioExecutor o--"*" ArbigentAgent ArbigentScenario o--"*" ArbigentAgentTask ArbigentProject o--"*" ArbigentScenario ``` ### Saved project file [Permalink: Saved project file](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#saved-project-file) Warning The yaml format is still under development and may change in the future. The project file is saved in YAML format and contains scenarios with goals, initialization methods, and cleanup data. Dependencies between scenarios are also defined. You can write a project file in YAML format by hand or create it using the Arbigent UI. The id is auto-generated UUID by Arbigent UI but you can change it to any string. #### Available Initialization Methods [Permalink: Available Initialization Methods](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#available-initialization-methods) - **Back**: Press the back button - **Wait**: Wait for a specified duration - **LaunchApp**: Launch an application - **CleanupData**: Clear application data - **OpenLink**: Open a URL/link - **MaestroYaml**: Execute a predefined Maestro YAML scenario - **Reconnect**: Disconnect and reconnect the device ``` scenarios: - id: "7788d7f4-7276-4cb3-8e98-7d3ad1d1cd47" goal: "Open the Now in Android app from the app list. The goal is to view the list\ \ of topics. Do not interact with the app beyond this." initializationMethods: - type: "CleanupData" packageName: "com.google.samples.apps.nowinandroid" - type: "LaunchApp" packageName: "com.google.samples.apps.nowinandroid" - type: "MaestroYaml" scenarioId: "login-flow-maestro" - id: "f0ef0129-c764-443f-897d-fc4408e5952b" goal: "In the Now in Android app, select an tech topic and complete the form in\ \ the \"For you\" tab. The goal is reached when articles are displayed. Do not\ \ click on any articles. If the browser opens, return to the app." dependency: "7788d7f4-7276-4cb3-8e98-7d3ad1d1cd47" imageAssertions: - assertionPrompt: "Articles are visible on the screen" - id: "73c785f7-0f45-4709-97b5-601b6803eb0d" goal: "Save an article using the Bookmark button." dependency: "f0ef0129-c764-443f-897d-fc4408e5952b" - id: "797514d2-fb04-4b92-9c07-09d46cd8f931" goal: "Check if a saved article appears in the Saved tab." dependency: "73c785f7-0f45-4709-97b5-601b6803eb0d" imageAssertions: - assertionPrompt: "The screen is showing Saved tab" - assertionPrompt: "There is an article in the screen" # Maestro YAML scenarios for initialization fixedScenarios: - id: "login-flow-maestro" title: "Login Flow" description: "Performs user login using predefined credentials" yamlText: | appId: com.google.samples.apps.nowinandroid --- - tapOn: "Sign In" - inputText: "test@example.com" - tapOn: "Password" - inputText: "password123" - tapOn: "Login" - assertVisible: "Welcome" ``` ## Code Interface [Permalink: Code Interface](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#code-interface) Warning The code interface is still under development and may change in the future. Arbigent provides a code interface for executing tests programmatically. Here's an example of how to run a test: ### Dependency [Permalink: Dependency](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#dependency) Stay tuned for the release of Arbigent on Maven Central. ### Running saved project yaml file [Permalink: Running saved project yaml file](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#running-saved-project-yaml-file) You can load a project yaml file and execute it using the following code: ``` class ArbigentTest { private val scenarioFile = File(this::class.java.getResource("/projects/nowinandroidsample.yaml").toURI()) @Test fun tests() = runTest( timeout = 10.minutes ) { val arbigentProject = ArbigentProject( file = scenarioFile, aiFactory = { OpenAIAi( apiKey = System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") ) }, deviceFactory = { AvailableDevice.Android( dadb = Dadb.discover()!! ).connectToDevice() } ) arbigentProject.execute() } } ``` ### Run a scenario directly [Permalink: Run a scenario directly](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#run-a-scenario-directly) ``` val agentConfig = AgentConfig { deviceFactory { FakeDevice() } ai(FakeAi()) } val arbigentScenarioExecutor = ArbigentScenarioExecutor { } val arbigentScenario = ArbigentScenario( id = "id2", agentTasks = listOf( ArbigentAgentTask("id1", "Login in the app and see the home tab.", agentConfig), ArbigentAgentTask("id2", "Search an episode and open detail", agentConfig) ), maxStepCount = 10, ) arbigentScenarioExecutor.execute( arbigentScenario ) ``` ### Run a goal directly [Permalink: Run a goal directly](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#run-a-goal-directly) ``` val agentConfig = AgentConfig { deviceFactory { FakeDevice() } ai(FakeAi()) } val task = ArbigentAgentTask("id1", "Login in the app and see the home tab.", agentConfig) ArbigentAgent(agentConfig) .execute(task) ``` ## About AI Agent for testing Android, iOS, and Web apps. Get Started in 5 Minutes. Arbigent's intuitive UI and powerful code interface make it accessible to everyone, while its scenario breakdown feature ensures scalability for even the most complex tasks. ### Topics [android](https://github.com/topics/android "Topic: android") [testing](https://github.com/topics/testing "Topic: testing") [ios](https://github.com/topics/ios "Topic: ios") [ai](https://github.com/topics/ai "Topic: ai") [gemini](https://github.com/topics/gemini "Topic: gemini") [openai-api](https://github.com/topics/openai-api "Topic: openai-api") [agentic](https://github.com/topics/agentic "Topic: agentic") [aiagent](https://github.com/topics/aiagent "Topic: aiagent") ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#readme-ov-file) ### License [Apache-2.0 license](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent#Apache-2.0-1-ov-file) ### Uh oh! There was an error while loading. [Please reload this page](https://github.com/takahirom/arbigent). ![](https://developer.apple.com/assets/elements/icons/symbols/gobackward5.svg)![](https://developer.apple.com/assets/elements/icons/symbols/goforward5.svg)

![](https://devimages-cdn.apple.com/wwdc-services/images/3055294D-836B-4513-B7B0-0BC5666246B0/10029/10029_wide_900x506_2x.jpg)

- # Record, replay, and review: UI automation with Xcode



Learn to record, run, and maintain XCUIAutomation tests in Xcode. Replay your XCTest UI tests in dozens of locales, device types, and system conditions using test plan configurations. Review your test results using the Xcode test report, and download screenshots and videos of your runs. We'll also cover best practices for preparing your app for automation with Accessibility and writing stable, high-quality automation code.


## Chapters



  - 0:00 - [Introduction and agenda](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=0)
  - 3:58 - [UI automation overview](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=238)
  - 6:26 - [Prepare your app for automation](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=386)
  - 11:32 - [Record your interactions](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=692)
  - 17:30 - [Replay in multiple configurations](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1050)
  - 20:54 - [Review videos and results](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1254)
  - 24:16 - [Next steps](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1456)

## Resources

  - [Performing accessibility testing for your app](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/Accessibility/performing-accessibility-testing-for-your-app)
  - [Improving code assessment by organizing tests into test plans](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/Xcode/organizing-tests-to-improve-feedback)
  - [Delivering an exceptional accessibility experience](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/Accessibility/delivering_an_exceptional_accessibility_experience)
    - [HD Video](https://devstreaming-cdn.apple.com/videos/wwdc/2025/344/6/d83ce906-0fb6-484b-a0f2-4f678161d5b8/downloads/wwdc2025-344_hd.mp4?dl=1)
    - [SD Video](https://devstreaming-cdn.apple.com/videos/wwdc/2025/344/6/d83ce906-0fb6-484b-a0f2-4f678161d5b8/downloads/wwdc2025-344_sd.mp4?dl=1)

## Related Videos

#### WWDC24

  - [Meet Swift Testing](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2024/10179)

#### WWDC23

  - [Build accessible apps with SwiftUI and UIKit](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2023/10036)
  - [Create practical workflows in Xcode Cloud](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2023/10278)
  - [Fix failures faster with Xcode test reports](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2023/10175)
  - [Perform accessibility audits for your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2023/10035)

#### WWDC22

  - [Author fast and reliable tests for Xcode Cloud](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2022/110361)

#### WWDC21

  - [SwiftUI Accessibility: Beyond the basics](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2021/10119)
- Search this video…






















0:06







[Hey, I’m Max, and I’m an engineer on the Xcode team.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=6) [There are so many awesome features and experiences inside of Xcode,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=10) [it can be overwhelming.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=14) [For example, did you know it’s possible to watch your app run on dozens of devices,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=15) [languages, and configurations with one click?](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=20) [And on top of that,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=23) [you can get a full quality video recording of every single run.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=24) [Well, that’s totally possible with the power of UI automation in Xcode.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=27) [Let's take a look at how that works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=31) [First, we’ll cover an overview of UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=33) [Then we’ll prepare your app, record your interactions as automation code,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=37) [replay the automation across several devices and languages,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=43) [and finally, watch video recordings of the results](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=46) [and see a report about whether each run passed or failed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=49)







0:54







[Let’s start with an overview of how UI automation works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=54)







0:58







[Inside Xcode, we have two testing frameworks: Swift Testing and XCTest.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=58) [Both frameworks are capable of quickly testing your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=63) [and your source code in many configurations.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=66)







1:10







[When you import XCTest,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=70) [a framework called XCUIAutomation is automatically included.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=72) [XCUIAutomation can be used to automate your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=76) [and interact with it like a person does.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=79) [These frameworks work together to provide a complete app testing suite,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=81) [and if you ask me, I think that’s pretty sweet.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=85)







1:29







[A complete app testing suite is usually comprised of both unit](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=89) [and UI automation tests.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=92) [Unit tests test your app’s logic and models, and with Swift testing,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=94) [it’s possible to run tests on frameworks](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=98) [and Swift packages that don’t even have a user interface.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=101)







1:45







[Meanwhile, UI automation tests validate your app’s user experience,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=105) [as well as its integration with Apple hardware](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=109) [and the behavior of common workflows.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=112)







1:55







[Generally, your test suite will have more unit tests than UI tests,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=115) [and you want to aim for full coverage of your code.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=119) [But UI automation tests let you see how your app looks,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=122) [behaves, and integrates with the rest of the operating system.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=125) [There are so many benefits to doing this.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=129)







2:12







[For example, you can test your app as a person would with gestures like taps,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=132) [swipes and clicks.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=136) [You can understand how your app is perceived](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=138) [by people who use assistive technologies](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=140) [like VoiceOver, Voice Control, and Dynamic Type.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=142)







2:26







[You can view your app running](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=146) [on all of the languages and regions that it supports](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=148) [and focus on languages that have large effects on the look of your app,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=150) [like languages with longer strings and languages with a right-to-left layout.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=154) [You can test your app’s integration with Apple hardware features,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=159) [like the Action button, camera button,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=162) [Apple TV remote, and Apple Watch’s Digital Crown.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=164) [And finally, you can test your app’s launch performance,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=168) [which is a key metric in understanding how quickly people can get started](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=171) [using your app.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=174) [To set up a UI automation workflow, there are three key phases:](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=176) [record, replay, and review.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=180)







3:03







[First, record your interactions like taps, swipes, and hardware button presses,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=183) [then watch Xcode automatically write those as code.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=189) [Then, replay your interactions across multiple devices,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=192) [languages, regions, and device orientations,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=196) [both on your devices and in Xcode Cloud.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=199) [Finally, review videos and results of your app running](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=202) [in all of those configurations and see which ones passed](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=206) [and which ones failed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=210)







3:32







[UI automation is supported on all Apple platforms:](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=212) [iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS (Designed for iPad).](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=215) [The same automation can even run on multiple platforms.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=222) [So it’s possible to build an automation once](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=225) [and run it on all your supported devices.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=228) [That means you can see how your app runs on Mac, iPhone and Vision Pro](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=230) [with one click and no code changes.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=235) [Let’s briefly go over how UI automation works.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=238)







4:01







[UI automation interacts with your app as a person does](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=241) [using gestures and hardware events.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=244) [Automation runs completely independently from your app,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=247) [so your app models and data are not directly accessible.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=250)







4:15







[UI automation tells the operating system which gestures to perform,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=255) [then synchronously waits for them to complete one at a time.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=259)







4:24







[These actions include launching your app, interacting with buttons and navigation,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=264) [setting system state like Dark Mode,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=268) [and even setting a simulated location for the device if you wanted to.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=270) [At Apple, accessibility is one of our core values.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=275) [Apple’s assistive technologies make sure that everyone can use your app](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=278) [regardless of any physical, visual, audible or motor impairment.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=282) [We work really hard to ensure that most of these technologies](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=287) [work with your app by default,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=290) [with no work needed from you as a developer.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=292) [That said, adding additional support can enrich your app’s experience](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=294) [and make it even easier to automate.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=299) [The thing is, accessibility is the underlying framework](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=302) [that powers UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=305) [Having a great accessibility experience](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=306) [means you get a great UI automation experience too.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=309)







5:12







[Accessibility provides information directly to UI automation,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=312) [such as element types, labels, values and frames](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=315) [but what accessibility sees](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=320) [is not necessarily one-to-one with what you see as a person.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=321)







5:26







[Let’s take a look at an example.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=326) [On this screen, the Great Barrier Reef button is visible in the UI](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=328) [but accessibility can see even more than that.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=333) [Sure, accessibility can see the elements type and label](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=336) [and expose them to UI automation](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=339) [but the identifier property is also exposed.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=341) [The accessibility identifier can be used to describe any element on screen uniquely,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=345) [relative to all the elements around it.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=349) [The identifier is not designed to be localized,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=352) [so you can use it to refer to the same UI element in any language or device.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=355)







6:00







[For checkboxes and other stateful elements,](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=360) [a value property can be used.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=363) [This reveals the element’s current state to both accessibility and UI automation.](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=365)







6:11

    // Add validations to the test case
    import XCTest

    class LandmarksUITests: XCTestCase {

      func testGreatBarrierAddedToFavorites() {
        let app = XCUIApplication()
        app.launch()
        app.cells["Landmark-186"].tap()
        XCTAssertTrue(
          app.staticTexts["Landmark-186"].waitForExistence(timeout: 10.0)),
          "Great Barrier exists"
        )

        let favoriteButton = app.buttons["Favorite"]
        favoriteButton.tap()
        XCTAssertTrue(
          favoriteButton.wait(for: \.value, toEqual: true, timeout: 10.0),
          "Great Barrier is a favorite"
        )
      }
    }

- - 0:00 - [Introduction and agenda](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=0)
  - Xcode's UI automation, powered by the XCUIAutomation framework, enables you to test your apps comprehensively. It works alongside Swift Testing and the XCTest framework, providing a complete app-testing suite. UI automation tests validate an app's user experience, its integration with Apple hardware, and the behavior of common workflows, complementing unit tests which focus on app logic and data models.

    The process involves three main phases:
    Recording interactions, which are then automatically converted into code.
    Replaying these interactions across various devices, languages, regions, and orientations.
    Reviewing video recordings and results of each UI test run.

    These automations allow you to test your apps as real users would, ensuring accessibility, language support, and hardware integration. It is supported on all Apple platforms, enabling you to build and run automation tests once for multiple devices, such as iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple TV, and Apple Watch, with a single click.

  - 3:58 - [UI automation overview](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=238)
  - UI automation mimics user interactions with an app, launching it, tapping buttons, and navigating through screens. It relies on the app's accessibility features — which provide labels, values, and unique identifiers for elements — to perform these actions independently. Accessibility, a core Apple value, ensures apps are usable by everyone and also powers UI automation.

  - 6:26 - [Prepare your app for automation](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=386)
  - To prepare the app automation, several steps are involved. First, add accessibility identifiers to the app's elements. These identifiers are unique labels written in SwiftUI, UIKit, or AppKit that help automation tools recognize and interact with specific parts of the app. They are particularly useful for elements with localized strings or dynamic content and need to be descriptive, static, and unique within the entire app.

    Next, conduct a quick review of the app's accessibility using Accessibility Inspector. This tool helps identify and fix any accessibility issues, ensuring that the app is well-prepared for automation. Accessibility Inspector allows you to view and modify accessibility properties for each view, making the app more usable for both assistive technologies and automation scripts.

    Finally, add a new UI testing target to the Xcode project. This target provides a dedicated space for storing the automation code. Once the target is set up, you can begin recording your interactions with the app, which will be automatically translated into Swift code, streamlining the automation process.

  - 11:32 - [Record your interactions](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=692)
  - The Landmarks sample project, shown in Xcode and Simulator, demonstrates planning a virtual vacation to Australia. UI recordings capture interactions within the app as code. The example adds a new collection, renames it "Max’s Australian Adventure," and populates it with landmarks like the Great Barrier Reef and Uluru.

    After the recording stops, the example reviews the generated code, selects appropriate UI queries, and adds validations using XCTest to ensure the app's functionality. The test is then rerun, successfully verifying the creation of the collection and the addition of landmarks. You can further enhance the tests by setting up specific device states, simulating locations, and performing accessibility audits.

  - 17:30 - [Replay in multiple configurations](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1050)
  - Xcode Cloud enables automated app testing in various configurations at-desk and in the cloud, as well as for different languages and devices. Test plans organize tests, set system settings, and manage properties. Configurations allow testing in specific locales, including languages with longer strings or right-to-left scripts.

    Xcode Cloud runs tests in the cloud, saving videos and screenshots of failures for review. An entire team can view run history, logs, and results, facilitating collaboration and remote monitoring. Advanced configurations and further details are available in Developer Documentation.

  - 20:54 - [Review videos and results](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1254)
  - The Xcode test report provides a comprehensive tool for analyzing test results. Upon identifying a failed test run, you can access video recordings and detailed descriptions. The report overlays UI interactions on the video and includes a timeline with failure diamonds for quick navigation.

    You can view present UI elements at the failure point, receive code suggestions, and directly edit your test code. After making corrections, the test can be rerun, and the report allows you to verify functionality across different languages and layouts, ultimately enhancing app quality and accessibility.

  - 24:16 - [Next steps](https://developer.apple.com/videos/play/wwdc2025/344/?time=1456)
  - For more information on unit testing and Swift testing, see "Meet Swift Testing" session from WWDC24; for questions or feedback, visit the Developer Forums.
